S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Write a tutoring prompt that requires you to produce an attempt before Claude responds
· Design a Socratic prompt that stages questions rather than delivering an explanation
· Build a misconception-check prompt that surfaces wrong beliefs and addresses them specifically
· Prompt Claude to give feedback on your reasoning rather than on your product
· Create a self-assessment loop that asks you to evaluate before Claude does
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: CORE CONCEPTS
blocks: The answer-first trap.
Active-constructive-interactive learning (Chi).
Staged hints.
Misconception diagnosis.
The Anthropic Learning Mode

data.title: BEFORE / AFTER PROMPT WALKTHROUGH.
blocks: · The prompt requires the learner to produce before Claude responds.
· It specifies feedback as specific rather than generic ("what I got right and wrong, specifically").
It stages the hint structure hints before full disclosure, not full disclosure first.
· It instructs Claude not to skip straight to the explanation.

S05.meta: // SLIDE 05 — THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST VERIFY
blocks: Verify by closing the tab and explaining.
Verify misconception diagnoses against an authoritative source.
Verify staged hints are actually staged.
Academic integrity.
data.section: THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Asking for explanations instead of asking for questions.
Treating fluent output as evidence of understanding.
Not specifying that feedback should be specific.
Asking Claude to generate practice problems without a difficulty ramp.
Using Claude to avoid hard thinking.
